title: A Message From Your Future Self (The Help You Need)
description: In this video, Sandeep breaks down five powerful mentorship models from around the world—Greek, Indian, Sufi, Japanese, and Yoruba—and what each one can teach us about finding the right mentor. Drawing from both ancient traditions and personal experience, he explains how different cultures approach growth, guidance, and learning, and offers practical takeaways for anyone seeking direction in their life or career.
transcript:
Most of us don't know what kind of
mentors we need. Why do we need them?
How do you even find them? Let's learn
from the five timeless mentorship
cultures around the world. One of them
might just change your life. We start
our journey in Greece. This 2400year-old
playbook still drives how Harvard builds
its future leaders. The very word mentor
came from Greek mythology. It comes from
the Odyssey. But it was Socrates who
rewrote the rules of mentorship. He
didn't see himself as a teacher, but a
midwife. Socrates mentored students by
being curious. Like, is stealing always
wrong? What if you steal to feed your
hungry child? Is true democracy ever
possible? The answers didn't matter to
him. What he wanted was his students to
become critical thinkers. That's how
Socrates mentored Plato who then
mentored Aristotle who then mentored
Alexander the Great. A single chain of
mentorship that can fundamentally
reshape the world. There is a more
recent example of this. In Silicon

trillion dollar coach and he mentored
the CEO of Apple, founders of Google, of

companies of course went on to change

Socratic model shows up everywhere

the world like Harvard or MIT or

questions. They don't give the answers.
They instead lead the discussion. The
method shows up in modern psychology as
well. It has become the foundation of

First, endless questioning can feel like

who are not used to it. Second, great
mentors are inherently curious. Now,
there are some mentors who know the
answers and still keep asking questions
until they get the answer they want.
That's not mentorship. That's actually
annoying. And perhaps most importantly,
there are studies that show that such
socratic methods in a group setting can
have a potential bias against women and
against those who are introverts. So
keep those risks in mind. And if you're
drawn to this kind of mentorship, here's

for someone who asks more questions than
they give answers. Number two, notice if
they say, "Yes, great, but why do you
think that?" That's a very good sign.
The mentor is interested in your

Arjun gets back up, puts on the battle
